title: Check if graph is bipartite (BFS coloring).
description: Check if graph is bipartite (BFS coloring).

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Initialize colors array of size 3 with -1 (unassigned). Start DFS from vertex 0.
Step 2: Assign color 0 to vertex 0. Move to vertex 1.
Step 3: Assign color 1 to vertex 1 (since adjacent to vertex 0 with color 0). Move to vertex 2.
Step 4: Assign color 0 to vertex 2 (since adjacent to vertex 1 with color 1). All vertices assigned.
Step 5: Check edges: edge [0,1] has colors 0 and 1 (valid), edge [1,2] has colors 1 and 0 (valid). All edges valid, return true.

Step 1: Initialize colors array of size 3 with -1. Start DFS.
Step 2: Try assignment: vertex 0=0, vertex 1=1, vertex 2=0. Check edges: [0,1] valid (0,1), [1,2] valid (1,0), [0,2] invalid (0,0). This assignment fails.
Step 3: Backtrack and try other assignments via DFS recursion. All possible assignments: 000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111.
Step 4: Check each: 000 fails (all edges invalid), 001 fails ([0,2] has 0,1 but [0,1] has 0,0 invalid), 010 fails ([0,1] has 0,1 valid but [1,2] has 1,0 valid, [0,2] has 0,0 invalid), 011 fails ([0,1] invalid 0,1), 100 fails ([0,1] invalid 1,0), 101 fails ([0,2] invalid 1,1), 110 fails ([1,2] invalid 1,0), 111 fails (all invalid).
Step 5: No valid assignment found, return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(2^n) with DFS
approaches.summary: Try all possible 2-color assignments for vertices using DFS, checking if any adjacent vertices share the same color. This is impractical for large graphs but conceptually simple.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n * m) where n is vertices and m is edges
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion stack and color storage

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Bipartite Check with DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if a graph is bipartite by trying all possible 2-color assignments using DFS.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the bipartite graph problem by systematically exploring all possible color assignments for vertices. The intuition is that a graph is bipartite if we can assign one of two colors (say 0 and 1) to each vertex such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color. Since there are n vertices, there are 2^n possible color assignments. We use DFS to generate all these assignments and check each one for validity. The algorithm starts with an empty color assignment and recursively assigns colors to vertices. At each step, we assign either color 0 or 1 to the current vertex, then recursively process the next vertex. When all vertices are assigned colors, we check if any adjacent vertices have the same color. If we find a valid assignment, the graph is bipartite; otherwise, it's not. Time complexity is O(2^n * m) because we generate 2^n assignments and for each assignment, we check all m edges. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and color storage. This approach is conceptually simple but impractical for large graphs due to exponential time complexity. It connects to the brute force paradigm where we exhaustively search all possibilities. Common mistakes include not pruning invalid assignments early (we could check adjacency constraints during assignment to improve efficiency) and misunderstanding the exponential nature of the solution. Real code pattern involves recursive DFS with backtracking.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize colors array of size 3 with -1 (unassigned). Start DFS from vertex 0.
Step 2: Assign color 0 to vertex 0. Move to vertex 1.
Step 3: Assign color 1 to vertex 1 (since adjacent to vertex 0 with color 0). Move to vertex 2.
Step 4: Assign color 0 to vertex 2 (since adjacent to vertex 1 with color 1). All vertices assigned.
Step 5: Check edges: edge [0,1] has colors 0 and 1 (valid), edge [1,2] has colors 1 and 0 (valid). All edges valid, return true.

Step 1: Initialize colors array of size 3 with -1. Start DFS.
Step 2: Try assignment: vertex 0=0, vertex 1=1, vertex 2=0. Check edges: [0,1] valid (0,1), [1,2] valid (1,0), [0,2] invalid (0,0). This assignment fails.
Step 3: Backtrack and try other assignments via DFS recursion. All possible assignments: 000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111.
Step 4: Check each: 000 fails (all edges invalid), 001 fails ([0,2] has 0,1 but [0,1] has 0,0 invalid), 010 fails ([0,1] has 0,1 valid but [1,2] has 1,0 valid, [0,2] has 0,0 invalid), 011 fails ([0,1] invalid 0,1), 100 fails ([0,1] invalid 1,0), 101 fails ([0,2] invalid 1,1), 110 fails ([1,2] invalid 1,0), 111 fails (all invalid).
Step 5: No valid assignment found, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS for Generating All Color Assignments
ladders.desc: DFS function that recursively generates all possible 2-color assignments for n vertices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for generating all 2^n possible color assignments using depth-first search. The intuition is to treat each vertex as a decision point where we can assign either color 0 or 1, and we explore all combinations recursively. The algorithm works by maintaining a current index representing which vertex we're assigning, and a colors array storing assignments. We start at index 0 and recursively call DFS for two branches: one where we assign color 0 to the current vertex, and another where we assign color 1. When we reach index n (all vertices assigned), we have a complete assignment. This is a classic backtracking pattern. Time complexity is O(2^n) since we generate 2^n assignments, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and colors array. This feeds into the full problem by providing the assignments that need to be checked for validity. Common mistakes include not handling the base case correctly (when index == n) and not copying the colors array properly in recursive calls (though we can pass it by reference with backtracking). Real code pattern uses recursion with a parameter for the current vertex index.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start DFS with index=0, colors=[-1,-1].
Step 2: At index 0, branch 1: assign color 0, colors=[0,-1], call DFS with index=1.
Step 3: At index 1, branch 1: assign color 0, colors=[0,0], index=2 (base case), record [0,0]. Backtrack.
Step 4: At index 1, branch 2: assign color 1, colors=[0,1], index=2, record [0,1]. Backtrack to index 0.
Step 5: At index 0, branch 2: assign color 1, colors=[1,-1], call DFS with index=1.
Step 6: At index 1, branch 1: assign color 0, colors=[1,0], index=2, record [1,0]. Backtrack.
Step 7: At index 1, branch 2: assign color 1, colors=[1,1], index=2, record [1,1]. Done.

Step 1: Start index=0, colors=[-1,-1,-1].
Step 2: Recursively explore: assign 0 to vertex 0, then explore vertices 1 and 2 similarly, generating 000, 001, 010, 011.
Step 3: Backtrack, assign 1 to vertex 0, then explore vertices 1 and 2, generating 100, 101, 110, 111.
Step 4: Total 2^3 = 8 assignments generated via DFS tree.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Validity of a Color Assignment
ladders.desc: Function that checks if a given color assignment satisfies the bipartite condition for all edges.
ladders.explanation: This core logic determines whether a specific color assignment makes the graph bipartite by verifying that no edge connects two vertices with the same color. The intuition is simple: for a graph to be bipartite, every edge must connect vertices of different colors. Given a colors array (where colors[i] is 0 or 1 for vertex i) and a list of edges, we iterate through all edges and check if the two endpoints have the same color. If any edge has endpoints with the same color, the assignment is invalid; otherwise, it's valid. This check is essential because the DFS generates assignments, but we need to filter out invalid ones. Time complexity is O(m) for checking m edges, and space complexity is O(1) aside from input storage. This feeds into the key sub-routine by being called for each complete assignment generated by DFS. Common mistakes include not handling cases where vertices might be unassigned (shouldn't happen in this context) or incorrectly comparing colors. Real code pattern is a simple loop over edges.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check edge [0,1]: colors[0]=0, colors[1]=1, different → valid.
Step 2: Check edge [1,2]: colors[1]=1, colors[2]=0, different → valid.
Step 3: All edges valid, return true.

Step 1: Check edge [0,1]: 0 and 1, valid.
Step 2: Check edge [1,2]: 1 and 0, valid.
Step 3: Check edge [0,2]: 0 and 0, same → invalid.
Step 4: Found invalid edge, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing a Graph with Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Creating and using an adjacency list representation for an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing the graph data structure, which is fundamental to the bipartite check. An adjacency list is a common representation where we have an array of lists, with each index corresponding to a vertex, and the list containing all vertices adjacent to it. For an undirected graph, if there's an edge between u and v, we add v to u's list and u to v's list. The intuition is that this representation allows efficient traversal of edges incident to a vertex. In the brute force approach, we use the adjacency list to check edges during validity checking. We construct it from the input edges list. Time complexity to build is O(n + m), and space complexity is O(n + m). This feeds into the core logic by providing the edges in a structured way for validity checking. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate edges, not making it undirected (adding both directions), or using inefficient representations like adjacency matrix for sparse graphs. Real code pattern initializes a list of lists for n vertices and appends neighbors.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize adj as list of 3 empty lists: [[], [], []].
Step 2: For edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0] → adj[0]=[1]; add 0 to adj[1] → adj[1]=[0].
Step 3: For edge [1,2]: add 2 to adj[1] → adj[1]=[0,2]; add 1 to adj[2] → adj[2]=[1].
Step 4: Result: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1].

Step 1: Initialize adj with 4 empty lists.
Step 2: Edge [0,1]: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0].
Step 3: Edge [1,2]: adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1].
Step 4: Edge [2,3]: adj[2]=[1,3], adj[3]=[2].
Step 5: Edge [3,0]: adj[3]=[2,0], adj[0]=[1,3].
Step 6: Final: adj[0]=[1,3], adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1,3], adj[3]=[2,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through All Edges of a Graph
ladders.desc: Looping over a list of edges to perform operations like checking colors.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing all edges in a graph, which is a fundamental step in graph algorithms. Given a list of edges where each edge is represented as a pair [u, v], we use a simple loop to iterate through each edge. The intuition is that to check properties like bipartiteness, we need to examine every connection in the graph. In the brute force approach, this operation is used in the validity check to ensure no edge violates the color constraint. The algorithm is straightforward: for each edge in the edges list, access the two vertices and perform the check. Time complexity is O(m) for m edges, and space complexity is O(1) for the iteration. This feeds into the building block as it's used when constructing the adjacency list and in the core logic for validity checking. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors if vertices are 1-indexed (but we assume 0-indexed here) or not handling empty edge lists. Real code pattern is a for-loop over the edges list.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize loop index i=0.
Step 2: Edge i=0: [0,1], extract u=0, v=1.
Step 3: Perform operation (e.g., check colors).
Step 4: Increment i to 1.
Step 5: Edge i=1: [1,2], extract u=1, v=2.
Step 6: Perform operation.
Step 7: Loop ends after i=1.

Step 1: For each edge in list: first [0,1], then [1,2], then [2,3].
Step 2: For each, access u and v and apply the required check or operation.
Step 3: After processing all 3 edges, done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Binary Choices (0/1 Assignment)
ladders.desc: Basic idea of assigning one of two values to each item in a set.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves the simple idea of making binary choices: for each of n items, we assign either value 0 or value 1. This is a fundamental combinatorial concept that underpins the brute force approach, as we need to try all possible assignments of two colors to vertices. The intuition is that with n items, there are 2^n possible assignments because each item has 2 independent choices. For example, with 2 items, the assignments are (0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1). We can think of this as a binary tree where each level represents an item, and branches represent choosing 0 or 1. This concept is essential because the brute force method relies on generating all these assignments to check which ones satisfy the bipartite condition. Time complexity for generating all assignments is O(2^n), and space complexity depends on how we store them. This feeds into the basic operation as it's the first step in the DFS sub-routine. Common mistakes include miscounting the number of assignments or not understanding the exponential growth. Real code pattern might involve recursion or iterative loops to generate combinations.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Item 1: choose 0 or 1.
Step 2: If choose 0 for item 1, then for item 2: choose 0 → 00, choose 1 → 01.
Step 3: If choose 1 for item 1, then for item 2: choose 0 → 10, choose 1 → 11.
Step 4: Total 2^2 = 4 assignments.

Step 1: For 3 items, each can be 0 or 1.
Step 2: List systematically: 000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111.
Step 3: This can be seen as binary numbers from 0 to 7.
Step 4: Total 2^3 = 8.

approaches.name: BFS Coloring O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use BFS to traverse the graph, assigning alternating colors to adjacent vertices. If a conflict arises (adjacent vertices have the same color), the graph is not bipartite. This handles disconnected graphs by checking each component.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(V) for queue and color array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bipartite Graph Detection via BFS Coloring
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph is bipartite using BFS with alternating color assignment.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph is one whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects vertices from different sets. This property is equivalent to the graph being 2-colorable, meaning we can assign one of two colors to each vertex so that no adjacent vertices share the same color. The BFS coloring approach leverages this by performing breadth-first traversal while maintaining a color array. We start BFS from each unvisited vertex to handle disconnected graphs. During BFS, we assign the current vertex a color (e.g., 0 or 1), then for each neighbor: if uncolored, assign the opposite color and enqueue; if already colored with the same color as current, we've found a conflict, proving the graph isn't bipartite. The algorithm runs in O(V+E) time as each vertex and edge is processed once, with O(V) space for the queue and color array. This connects to L1 by integrating the BFS traversal with color checking. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs (must check all components), using DFS incorrectly (BFS ensures level-by-level coloring), and forgetting to initialize colors properly. Real code pattern: initialize color array with -1 (uncolored), iterate through vertices, if uncolored start BFS, assign color 0, process queue, for each neighbor check colors, return false on conflict, else true after all vertices.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0: color[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process 0: neighbors 1,3. color[1]=1, color[3]=1, queue=[1,3]. Step 3: Process 1: neighbors 0,2. color[0]=0 (matches, ok), color[2]=0 (opposite of 1's color 1), queue=[3,2]. Step 4: Process 3: neighbors 0,2. color[0]=0 (matches, ok), color[2]=0 (matches 3's color 1, ok), queue=[2]. Step 5: Process 2: neighbors 1,3. color[1]=1 (opposite of 2's color 0, ok), color[3]=1 (opposite, ok). All vertices colored, no conflicts, return true.

Step 1: colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at 0: color[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process 0: neighbors 1,2,3. color[1]=1, color[2]=1, color[3]=1, queue=[1,2,3]. Step 3: Process 1: neighbors 0,2. color[0]=0 (ok), color[2]=1 (conflict: 2 has same color 1 as 1). Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Color Assignment for a Component
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to traverse a connected component, assigning alternating colors and detecting conflicts.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the bipartite detection algorithm. Given a starting vertex and the graph's adjacency list, it performs BFS to explore all vertices in that component. The intuition is that BFS processes vertices level-by-level, which naturally suits alternating color assignment: vertices at even depths get one color, odd depths get the other. We maintain a queue for BFS and a color array (initialized externally). Start by coloring the start vertex with color 0 and enqueue it. While queue not empty, dequeue a vertex, examine its neighbors: if a neighbor is uncolored (color -1), assign the opposite color (1 - current color) and enqueue it; if already colored with the same color as current, return false (conflict). If BFS completes without conflicts, return true. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the component, space O(V) for queue. This feeds into L0 by being called for each unvisited component. Common mistakes: not using a queue (stack leads to DFS, which works but BFS is more intuitive), incorrect color toggling (use 1-color), and missing conflict check for already visited neighbors. Real code pattern: define function bfs(start, graph, colors): queue=[start], colors[start]=0, while queue: node=queue.pop(0), for neighbor in graph[node]: if colors[neighbor]==-1: colors[neighbor]=1-colors[node], queue.append(neighbor); elif colors[neighbor]==colors[node]: return false; return true.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process 0: neighbors 1,2. colors[1]=1, colors[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Process 1: neighbors 0,3. colors[0]=0 (ok), colors[3]=0, queue=[2,3]. Step 4: Process 2: neighbors 0,3. colors[0]=0 (ok), colors[3]=0 (ok). Step 5: Process 3: neighbors 1,2. colors[1]=1 (opposite of 3's 0, ok), colors[2]=1 (opposite, ok). Queue empty, return true.

Step 1: colors[0]=0, queue=[0]. Step 2: Process 0: neighbors 1,2. colors[1]=1, colors[2]=1, queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Process 1: neighbors 0,2. colors[0]=0 (ok), colors[2]=1 (conflict with 1's color 1). Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Color Assignment During Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: The technique of assigning two colors alternately to vertices during BFS/DFS to check for bipartiteness.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a graph is bipartite if and only if it is 2-colorable with no adjacent vertices sharing the same color. During graph traversal (BFS or DFS), we can maintain this property by assigning colors in an alternating pattern. Start with an arbitrary vertex, color it 0. As we traverse to neighbors, assign the opposite color (1). Continue recursively: each time we move to a new vertex, flip the color. If we encounter a neighbor already visited, check if its color matches the current vertex's color; if yes, a conflict exists, and the graph is not bipartite. This works because bipartite graphs have no odd-length cycles, and the alternating assignment ensures even cycles maintain consistency while odd cycles cause conflicts. The logic is independent of traversal order but BFS is preferred for clarity. Time and space depend on traversal but are O(V+E) and O(V). This feeds into L1 as the central mechanism within BFS. Common mistakes: using more than two colors, not handling initial uncolored state, and incorrect conflict detection (should check equality, not inequality). Real code pattern: during traversal, for current vertex u with color c, for each neighbor v: if uncolored, set color[v]=1-c; else if color[v]==c, return false.

examples.input: Traversal order: 0->1->2->3, graph edges: 0-1, 1-2, 2-3, initial color 0 at vertex 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, color=0. Step 2: Move to 1 via edge 0-1, assign opposite color 1. Step 3: Move to 2 via edge 1-2, assign opposite color 0. Step 4: Move to 3 via edge 2-3, assign opposite color 1. All adjacent pairs (0-1,1-2,2-3) have different colors, so valid.

examples.input: Traversal order: 0->1->2->0 (cycle), graph edges: 0-1, 1-2, 2-0, initial color 0 at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, color=0. Step 2: Move to 1 via 0-1, color=1. Step 3: Move to 2 via 1-2, color=0. Step 4: Check edge 2-0: vertex 2 color=0, vertex 0 color=0, same color conflict. Graph has odd cycle (triangle), not bipartite.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal (BFS) on an Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Implement breadth-first search to visit all vertices in a connected graph component.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores vertices level by level from a starting point. It uses a queue to manage the order of visitation. The intuition is to visit all neighbors of the current vertex before moving to their neighbors, ensuring shortest paths in unweighted graphs. For an adjacency list representation, start by enqueuing the start vertex and marking it visited. While the queue is not empty, dequeue a vertex, process it (e.g., print or store), then enqueue all its unvisited neighbors and mark them visited. This ensures each vertex is processed exactly once. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is considered, space is O(V) for the queue and visited set. This feeds into L2 as the traversal framework where color assignment is integrated. Common mistakes: using a stack instead of queue (turns into DFS), not marking visited when enqueuing (causes repeats), and infinite loops in cyclic graphs. Real code pattern: def bfs(start, graph): visited=set(), queue=[start], visited.add(start); while queue: node=queue.pop(0); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor), queue.append(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: visited={}, queue=[0], visited={0}. Step 2: dequeue 0, process 0, neighbors 1,2: add to visited and queue: visited={0,1,2}, queue=[1,2]. Step 3: dequeue 1, process 1, neighbors 0,3: 0 visited, add 3: visited={0,1,2,3}, queue=[2,3]. Step 4: dequeue 2, process 2, neighbors 0,3: both visited. Step 5: dequeue 3, process 3, neighbors 1,2: both visited. Queue empty, done.

Step 1: visited={}, queue=[0], visited={0}. Step 2: dequeue 0, process 0, neighbor 1: add 1, visited={0,1}, queue=[1]. Step 3: dequeue 1, process 1, neighbors 0,2: 0 visited, add 2: visited={0,1,2}, queue=[2]. Step 4: dequeue 2, process 2, neighbor 1: visited. Queue empty, done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Management for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement and use a queue data structure to manage the order of vertex processing in BFS.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structure essential for BFS. It ensures that vertices are processed in the order they are discovered, which corresponds to level-by-level traversal. Basic operations include enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front). In BFS, we start by enqueuing the starting vertex. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a vertex, process it, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This guarantees that all vertices at distance k are processed before any at distance k+1. Implementation can use a list (with pop(0) for dequeue and append for enqueue) or collections.deque for efficiency. Time complexity for queue operations is O(1) per enqueue/dequeue, contributing to overall O(V+E). Space is O(V) for queue storage. This feeds into L3 as the core mechanism for BFS traversal. Common mistakes: using pop() instead of pop(0) (makes it a stack), not checking queue emptiness, and enqueuing duplicates. Real code pattern: queue=[start], while queue: node=queue.pop(0), process node, for neighbor in neighbors: if not visited: queue.append(neighbor).

examples.input: Start vertex: 0, neighbors added in order: after processing 0, add 1 and 2; after processing 1, add 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial queue=[0]. Step 2: Dequeue 0, process, add neighbors 1,2: queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, process, add neighbor 3: queue=[2,3]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, process, no new neighbors: queue=[3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3, process, queue empty.

examples.input: Start vertex: A, graph: A connected to B and C, B connected to D
examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[A]. Step 2: dequeue A, add B,C: queue=[B,C]. Step 3: dequeue B, add D: queue=[C,D]. Step 4: dequeue C, no add: queue=[D]. Step 5: dequeue D, queue empty.

testCases.input: Start: 0, add after dequeue 0: [1,2], after dequeue 1: [3], after dequeue 2: []
Start: A, add after A: [B], after B: [C], after C: []
Start: 1, add after 1: [2,3], after 2: [4], after 3: [5], after 4: [], after 5: []
Start: X, add after X: [Y,Z], after Y: [], after Z: []
Start: 0, add after 0: [1], after 1: [2], after 2: [3], after 3: []
Start: P, add after P: [Q,R,S], after Q: [T], after R: [], after S: [], after T: []
Start: alpha, add after alpha: [beta,gamma], after beta: [delta], after gamma: [], after delta: []
Start: 5, add after 5: [6,7,8], after 6: [9], after 7: [], after 8: [], after 9: []
Start: root, add after root: [left,right], after left: [leftChild], after right: [rightChild], after leftChild: [], after rightChild: []

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation Using Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Understand and create an adjacency list to represent an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a way to represent a graph where each vertex has a list of its adjacent vertices (neighbors). For an undirected graph, if vertex u is connected to vertex v, then v appears in u's list and u appears in v's list. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs, using O(V+E) space. To create it, start with a list of lists of size V (number of vertices), initialized empty. For each edge (u,v), append v to the list at index u, and append u to the list at index v. This ensures symmetry for undirected graphs. The intuition is that it directly captures connectivity, making traversal algorithms like BFS straightforward. Time to build is O(E). This feeds into L4 as the input structure for BFS. Common mistakes: forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, using incorrect indices, and not initializing lists properly. Real code pattern: V=number of vertices, graph=[[] for _ in range(V)]; for each edge (u,v): graph[u].append(v), graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge [0,1]: append 1 to graph[0] -> [[1],[],[]]; append 0 to graph[1] -> [[1],[0],[]]. Step 3: Add edge [1,2]: append 2 to graph[1] -> [[1],[0,2],[]]; append 1 to graph[2] -> [[1],[0,2],[1]]. Final graph.

approaches.name: DFS Coloring O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use DFS recursively or iteratively to assign alternating colors, checking for conflicts during traversal. Similar to BFS but explores as deep as possible first, which can be more intuitive for some graph structures.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(V) for recursion stack or stack and color array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Graph is Bipartite Using DFS Coloring
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of DFS-based bipartite graph checking with color assignment and conflict detection.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires determining whether an undirected graph is bipartite using DFS coloring. A bipartite graph can have its vertices divided into two disjoint sets such that no two vertices within the same set are adjacent. The DFS approach explores the graph depth-first, assigning alternating colors (e.g., 0 and 1) to vertices. Starting from an uncolored vertex, we assign it color 0, then recursively visit all its neighbors. For each neighbor, if it's uncolored, we assign the opposite color and continue DFS; if already colored, we check if it conflicts with the expected color. A conflict occurs when two adjacent vertices have the same color, indicating the graph is not bipartite. The algorithm must handle disconnected graphs by initiating DFS from each unvisited component. The intuition is that DFS naturally explores connected components deeply, making it efficient for detecting cycles that violate bipartiteness. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a color array with -1 (uncolored). 2) For each vertex, if uncolored, start DFS with color 0. 3) In DFS, for each neighbor: if uncolored, assign opposite color and recurse; if colored and same as current, return false. 4) If DFS returns false anywhere, the graph is not bipartite; otherwise, it is. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the color array and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This level integrates the DFS helper function with graph traversal. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs, forgetting to check all neighbors, or incorrect color assignment. Real code pattern: def isBipartite(graph): colors = [-1]*len(graph); for v in range(len(graph)): if colors[v]==-1: if not dfs(v,0): return False; return True; def dfs(v,color): colors[v]=color; for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor]==-1: if not dfs(neighbor,1-color): return False; elif colors[neighbor]==color: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: Start at vertex 0 (uncolored), call dfs(0,0). Step 3: In dfs(0,0): set colors[0]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 3. For neighbor 1: colors[1]==-1, call dfs(1,1). In dfs(1,1): set colors[1]=1. Neighbors: 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 !=1, no conflict. For neighbor 2: colors[2]==-1, call dfs(2,0). In dfs(2,0): set colors[2]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 3. For neighbor 1: colors[1]==1 !=0, no conflict. For neighbor 3: colors[3]==-1, call dfs(3,1). In dfs(3,1): set colors[3]=1. Neighbors: 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 !=1. For neighbor 2: colors[2]==0 !=1. dfs(3,1) returns True. dfs(2,0) returns True. dfs(1,1) returns True. Back to dfs(0,0): for neighbor 3: colors[3]==1 !=0, no conflict. dfs(0,0) returns True. Step 4: All vertices visited, return True.

Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: Start at vertex 0 (uncolored), call dfs(0,0). Step 3: In dfs(0,0): set colors[0]=0. Neighbors: 1,2,3. For neighbor 1: colors[1]==-1, call dfs(1,1). In dfs(1,1): set colors[1]=1. Neighbors: 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 !=1. For neighbor 2: colors[2]==-1, call dfs(2,0). In dfs(2,0): set colors[2]=0. Neighbors: 0,1,3. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 ==0 (conflict with expected 1? Wait, in dfs(2,0), current color is 0, neighbor 0 has color 0, but they are adjacent, so conflict). Actually, check: In dfs(2,0), neighbor 0 has color 0, which equals current color 0, so return False. This propagates up, and isBipartite returns False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Helper Function for Color Assignment and Conflict Check
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive DFS function that assigns colors and detects conflicts in a connected component.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core DFS function used in the bipartite checking algorithm. It takes a vertex and its assigned color as input, and recursively visits all neighbors to ensure bipartite conditions hold. The intuition is that DFS allows deep exploration of the graph, making it natural to propagate colors along paths and detect cycles that cause conflicts. Step-by-step: 1) Mark the current vertex with the given color. 2) For each neighbor: if uncolored, recursively call DFS with the opposite color; if already colored, check if it matches the current color—if yes, a conflict is found, return false. 3) If all neighbors are processed without conflict, return true. Time complexity for this function on a connected component is O(V+E) in worst case, but typically O(degree of start vertex + edges in component). Space complexity is O(V) due to recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main algorithm for each unvisited vertex. Common mistakes include not passing the correct opposite color, infinite recursion in cycles, or missing conflict checks. Real code pattern: def dfs(v,color): colors[v]=color; for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor]==-1: if not dfs(neighbor,1-color): return False; elif colors[neighbor]==color: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors[0]=0. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: colors[1]==-1, call dfs(1,1). In dfs(1,1): colors[1]=1. Neighbors: 0 and 3. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 !=1. For neighbor 3: colors[3]==-1, call dfs(3,0). In dfs(3,0): colors[3]=0. Neighbors: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: colors[1]==1 !=0. For neighbor 2: colors[2]==-1, call dfs(2,1). In dfs(2,1): colors[2]=1. Neighbors: 0 and 3. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 !=1. For neighbor 3: colors[3]==0 !=1. dfs(2,1) returns True. dfs(3,0) returns True. dfs(1,1) returns True. Back to dfs(0,0): for neighbor 2: colors[2]==1 !=0. dfs(0,0) returns True.

Step 1: colors[0]=0. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: colors[1]==-1, call dfs(1,1). In dfs(1,1): colors[1]=1. Neighbors: 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 !=1. For neighbor 2: colors[2]==-1, call dfs(2,0). In dfs(2,0): colors[2]=0. Neighbors: 0 and 1. For neighbor 0: colors[0]==0 ==0 (conflict), return False. This propagates up, and dfs(0,0) returns False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Color Assignment and Conflict Detection in Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the logic of assigning alternating colors during graph traversal and checking for adjacent same-color vertices.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight of bipartite checking: assigning two colors (e.g., 0 and 1) to vertices such that no edge connects vertices of the same color. The logic involves traversing the graph (via DFS or BFS) and for each vertex, assigning a color, then ensuring all neighbors have the opposite color. If a neighbor already has a color, it must differ from the current vertex's color; otherwise, a conflict is detected, indicating the graph is not bipartite. The intuition is that bipartite graphs are 2-colorable, and this property can be verified by attempting to 2-color the graph during traversal. Step-by-step: 1) Start with an uncolored vertex, assign color 0. 2) For each neighbor: if uncolored, assign color 1 (opposite), and continue; if colored, check if color equals current vertex's color—if yes, conflict. 3) Repeat for all vertices in the connected component. Time complexity for this logic on a graph is O(V+E) as each edge is checked once. Space complexity is O(V) for storing colors. Connection to L1: This logic is encapsulated in the DFS helper function. Common mistakes include incorrect color alternation (e.g., not using 1-color) or missing conflict checks for already colored neighbors. Real code pattern: color = 0; for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor]==-1: colors[neighbor]=1-color; else if colors[neighbor]==color: return False.

examples.input: graph = [[1,2],[0,3],[0,3],[1,2]], start vertex=0, colors array initially all -1
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, assign color 0. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: uncolored, assign 1. For neighbor 2: uncolored, assign 1. Step 3: Process vertex 1 (color 1): neighbors 0 and 3. For neighbor 0: colored 0 !=1, okay. For neighbor 3: uncolored, assign 0. Step 4: Process vertex 2 (color 1): neighbors 0 and 3. For neighbor 0: colored 0 !=1. For neighbor 3: colored 0 !=1. Step 5: Process vertex 3 (color 0): neighbors 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: colored 1 !=0. For neighbor 2: colored 1 !=0. Final colors: [0,1,1,0], no conflicts.

examples.input: graph = [[1,2],[0,2],[0,1]], start vertex=0, colors array initially all -1
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, assign color 0. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: uncolored, assign 1. For neighbor 2: uncolored, assign 1. Step 3: Process vertex 1 (color 1): neighbors 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: colored 0 !=1, okay. For neighbor 2: colored 1 ==1 (conflict with current color 1? Wait, current vertex is 1 with color 1, neighbor 2 has color 1, so adjacent same color, conflict detected). Actually, in logic: when processing vertex 1, check neighbor 2: colors[2]==1 equals current color 1, so conflict.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Using Depth-First Search (DFS)
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to visit all vertices in a connected component of an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This level covers DFS traversal, a fundamental graph algorithm used as a building block for bipartite checking. DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, making it suitable for detecting cycles and exploring connected components. The intuition is to start at a vertex, mark it as visited, and recursively visit all its unvisited neighbors. For an undirected graph, we use an adjacency list representation. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a visited array with False. 2) Define a recursive function dfs(v): mark v as visited, then for each neighbor in graph[v], if not visited, call dfs(neighbor). 3) To handle disconnected graphs, iterate through all vertices and start DFS from unvisited ones. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L2: DFS traversal is extended by adding color assignment and conflict checks. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (leading to infinite loops) or missing disconnected components. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*n; def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for neighbor in graph[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor); for v in range(n): if not visited[v]: dfs(v).

examples.trace: Step 1: visited = [F,F,F,F]. Step 2: Start dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1). In dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: 0 and 3. For neighbor 0: visited, skip. For neighbor 3: not visited, call dfs(3). In dfs(3): mark visited[3]=True. Neighbors: 1 and 2. For neighbor 1: visited, skip. For neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2). In dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: 0 and 3. Both visited, return. Back to dfs(3), return. Back to dfs(1), return. Back to dfs(0): for neighbor 2: visited, skip. dfs(0) returns. Final visited: [T,T,T,T].

Step 1: visited = [F,F,F]. Step 2: Start dfs(0): mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: 1. For neighbor 1: not visited, call dfs(1). In dfs(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: visited, skip. For neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2). In dfs(2): mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: 1. Visited, return. Back to dfs(1), return. Back to dfs(0), return. Final visited: [T,T,T].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Graph Adjacency List and Managing State Arrays
ladders.desc: Perform basic operations on graph data structures: accessing neighbors from an adjacency list and updating state arrays (e.g., visited or colors).
ladders.explanation: This level involves fundamental operations required for graph algorithms: representing a graph as an adjacency list, iterating through neighbors of a vertex, and maintaining state arrays like visited or color arrays. The intuition is that graphs are often stored as lists of lists, where graph[i] contains all vertices adjacent to i. Basic operations include: initializing an array of size V, accessing graph[v] to get neighbors, and updating array values. Step-by-step: 1) Define graph as a list of lists, e.g., graph = [[1,2],[0,3],[0,3],[1,2]]. 2) Initialize state array: state = [-1]*len(graph) for colors or [False]*len(graph) for visited. 3) For a vertex v, iterate for neighbor in graph[v]: perform operations like checking state[neighbor] or updating it. Time complexity for iterating neighbors is O(degree(v)). Space complexity is O(V) for state arrays. Connection to L3: These operations are used in DFS traversal. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds, incorrect array initialization, or not handling empty graphs. Real code pattern: graph = [[1,2],[0,3],[0,3],[1,2]]; state = [-1]*4; for v in range(4): for neighbor in graph[v]: if state[neighbor]==-1: state[neighbor]=0.

examples.input: graph = [[1,2],[0,3],[0,3],[1,2]], vertex=0, state array initially all -1
examples.trace: Step 1: graph[0] = [1,2]. Step 2: Initialize state = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Step 3: For vertex 0, iterate neighbors: neighbor 1: state[1]==-1, set state[1]=0. neighbor 2: state[2]==-1, set state[2]=0. State becomes [-1,0,0,-1].

examples.input: graph = [[1],[0,2],[1]], vertex=1, state array initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: graph[1] = [0,2]. Step 2: Initialize state = [False,False,False]. Step 3: For vertex 1, iterate neighbors: neighbor 0: state[0]==False, set state[0]=True. neighbor 2: state[2]==False, set state[2]=True. Also, mark state[1]=True. State becomes [True,True,True].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Graphs and Adjacency List Representation
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of graph theory: vertices, edges, and how to represent an undirected graph using an adjacency list.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces foundational concepts for graph algorithms. A graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). An undirected graph has edges that do not have a direction, meaning if vertex A is connected to vertex B, then B is also connected to A. The adjacency list is a common representation where each vertex has a list of its adjacent vertices. For example, graph = [[1,2],[0,3],[0,3],[1,2]] represents a graph with 4 vertices: vertex 0 connected to 1 and 2, vertex 1 connected to 0 and 3, etc. The intuition is that this representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs and allows quick access to neighbors. Step-by-step: 1) Identify vertices (usually numbered 0 to V-1). 2) For each vertex, list all vertices it is connected to. 3) Ensure symmetry for undirected graphs: if A is in B's list, B must be in A's list. Time complexity for accessing neighbors is O(degree(v)). Space complexity is O(V+E). Connection to L4: This representation is used for iterating over neighbors. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing, missing edges in undirected graphs, or not handling empty lists. Real code pattern: graph = []; graph.append([1,2]) for vertex 0; graph.append([0,3]) for vertex 1; etc.

examples.trace: Step 1: Vertex 0 is connected to 1, so graph[0] = [1]. Step 2: Vertex 1 is connected to 0 and 2, so graph[1] = [0,2]. Step 3: Vertex 2 is connected to 1, so graph[2] = [1]. Final: [[1],[0,2],[1]].

Step 1: Vertex 0 connected to 1 and 2: graph[0] = [1,2]. Step 2: Vertex 1 connected to 0 and 3: graph[1] = [0,3]. Step 3: Vertex 2 connected to 0 and 3: graph[2] = [0,3]. Step 4: Vertex 3 connected to 1 and 2: graph[3] = [1,2]. Final: [[1,2],[0,3],[0,3],[1,2]].

approaches.name: Union-Find with Bipartite Check O(E α(V))
approaches.summary: Use a disjoint set union (Union-Find) structure to group vertices into two sets based on edges, ensuring no edge connects vertices within the same set. This method avoids explicit graph traversal and uses union operations for efficiency.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(E α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(V) for parent and rank arrays

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bipartite Graph Check with Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution using Union-Find to determine if a graph is bipartite
ladders.explanation: This approach uses a modified Union-Find structure to check if a graph is bipartite without explicit graph traversal. The key insight is that in a bipartite graph, vertices can be partitioned into two sets such that all edges connect vertices from different sets. We implement this by maintaining two groups for each vertex: one representing the vertex itself and another representing its 'opposite' (the set it cannot be in). For each edge (u, v), we union u with the opposite of v and v with the opposite of u. If at any point u and v are in the same set (meaning they must be in opposite sets but the edge connects them), the graph is not bipartite. The algorithm processes all edges in O(E α(V)) time using path compression and union by rank. Space complexity is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This method is efficient and avoids the need for BFS/DFS traversal, making it suitable for large graphs. Common mistakes include not initializing the opposite sets correctly or forgetting to check if u and v are already in the same set before union operations, which could lead to false negatives. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the union-find operations implemented in L1 to manage sets efficiently.

examples.trace: Initialize Union-Find with 8 elements (4 vertices + 4 opposites). Process edge (0,1): union(0, opp(1)=5) and union(1, opp(0)=4). Sets: {0,5}, {1,4}. Process edge (0,3): union(0, opp(3)=7) and union(3, opp(0)=4). Sets: {0,5,7}, {1,3,4}. Process edge (1,2): union(1, opp(2)=6) and union(2, opp(1)=5). Sets: {0,2,5,7}, {1,3,4,6}. Process edge (2,3): union(2, opp(3)=7) and union(3, opp(2)=6). Sets: {0,2,3,5,6,7}, {1,4}. Check if 2 and 3 are in same set: find(2)=0, find(3)=0, same set but edge connects them? Actually, we check before union: for edge (2,3), find(2)=0, find(3)=0, they are already in same set, meaning graph is not bipartite? Wait, correction: We should check if u and v are in same set (meaning they must be in opposite sets but edge connects them). Here, find(2)=0, find(3)=0, so they are in same set, which violates bipartite condition. But expected output is true. Let's re-trace carefully. Initialize parent[i]=i for i=0..7, rank[i]=0. opp(i)=i+4. Edge (0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, not same set. union(0,5): parent[5]=0. union(1,4): parent[4]=1. Edge (0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=3, not same set. union(0,7): parent[7]=0. union(3,4): find(3)=3, find(4)=1, not same set, union by rank: parent[3]=1. Edge (1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=2, not same set. union(1,6): parent[6]=1. union(2,5): find(2)=2, find(5)=0, not same set, union: parent[2]=0. Edge (2,3): find(2)=0, find(3)=1, not same set. union(2,7): find(2)=0, find(7)=0, same set, so no op. union(3,6): find(3)=1, find(6)=1, same set, no op. Processed all edges without conflict, return true.

Initialize Union-Find with 8 elements. opp(i)=i+4. Edge (0,1): union(0,5), union(1,4). Sets: {0,5}, {1,4}. Edge (0,2): union(0,6), union(2,4). Sets: {0,5,6}, {1,2,4}. Edge (0,3): union(0,7), union(3,4). Sets: {0,5,6,7}, {1,2,3,4}. Edge (1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=1, they are in same set, but edge connects them, so return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union-Find Operations with Opposite Sets
ladders.desc: Implement find and union operations for managing bipartite partitions
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core Union-Find operations (find and union) adapted for bipartite checking. We maintain two sets per vertex: the vertex itself and its 'opposite' set (vertex + V). The find operation uses path compression to efficiently determine the root of a set. The union operation merges two sets using union by rank to keep the tree flat. For bipartite checking, we union a vertex with the opposite of its neighbor. The intuition is that if u is connected to v, then u must be in the opposite set of v. By unioning u with opp(v), we enforce this constraint. Time complexity for each find/union is O(α(V)) due to path compression and union by rank. Space complexity is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This feeds into L0 by providing the efficient set management needed to process all edges. Common mistakes include not initializing opposite sets correctly or using inefficient find without path compression. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(2*V)), rank = [0]*(2*V); def find(x): if parent[x]!=x: parent[x]=find(parent[x]); return parent[x]; def union(x,y): rx,ry=find(x),find(y); if rx==ry: return; if rank[rx]<rank[ry]: parent[rx]=ry; elif rank[rx]>rank[ry]: parent[ry]=rx; else: parent[ry]=rx; rank[rx]+=1.

examples.input: V=3, edges=[(0,1),(1,2)], perform unions: union(0,opp(1)), union(1,opp(0)), union(1,opp(2)), union(2,opp(1))
examples.trace: Initialize parent[0..5]=[0,1,2,3,4,5], rank all 0. opp(i)=i+3. Edge (0,1): union(0,4): find(0)=0, find(4)=4, union by rank: parent[4]=0, rank[0]=1. union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, union: parent[3]=1, rank[1]=1. Edge (1,2): union(1,5): find(1)=1, find(5)=5, union: parent[5]=1. union(2,4): find(2)=2, find(4)=0, union: parent[2]=0. Final find: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, find(2)=0, find(3)=1, find(4)=0, find(5)=1.

examples.input: V=2, edges=[(0,1)], perform unions: union(0,opp(1)), union(1,opp(0))
examples.trace: Initialize parent[0..3]=[0,1,2,3], rank all 0. opp(i)=i+2. Edge (0,1): union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=3, union: parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1. union(1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=2, union: parent[2]=1, rank[1]=1. Final find: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, find(2)=1, find(3)=0.

testCases.input: V=3, edges=[(0,1),(1,2)], union(0,opp(1)) then union(1,opp(0)) then find(0)==find(1)
V=3, edges=[(0,1),(1,2)], union(0,opp(1)) then union(1,opp(2)) then find(opp(0))==find(2)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Enforcing Bipartite Constraints with Set Unions
ladders.desc: The central technique of unioning vertices with opposites to prevent same-set edges
ladders.explanation: The core logic of this approach is to enforce bipartite constraints by unioning each vertex with the opposite set of its neighbors. For an edge (u, v), we perform union(u, opp(v)) and union(v, opp(u)). This ensures that u and v are placed in different sets because u is grouped with the opposite of v, and v with the opposite of u. If at any point u and v are found to be in the same set (i.e., find(u) == find(v)), it means a contradiction has occurred: u must be in the opposite set of v, but they are connected by an edge, so the graph is not bipartite. The intuition is that we are building two partitions dynamically: one set for 'group A' and another for 'group B', but we represent them indirectly through union operations. This method avoids explicit coloring and uses set membership to track constraints. Time complexity is O(E) for processing edges, with each union/find taking O(α(V)). Space is O(V) for the Union-Find structure. This feeds into L1 by providing the logical rules that guide the union operations. Common mistakes include not checking for find(u) == find(v) before unioning or misunderstanding the opposite set indexing. Real code pattern: for u in range(V): for v in graph[u]: if find(u) == find(v): return False; union(u, opp(v)); union(v, opp(u)).

examples.input: Graph with V=3, edges: 0-1, 1-2. Apply core logic step-by-step.
examples.trace: Initialize Union-Find for 6 elements (V=3, opp(i)=i+3). Edge (0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, not same. union(0,4): sets {0,4}. union(1,3): sets {1,3}. Edge (1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=2, not same. union(1,5): sets {1,3,5}. union(2,4): find(2)=2, find(4)=0, not same, union: sets {0,2,4}. All edges processed without find(u)==find(v), so bipartite.

examples.input: Graph with V=3, edges: 0-1, 1-2, 0-2. Apply core logic.
examples.trace: Initialize as above. Edge (0,1): union(0,4), union(1,3). Edge (1,2): union(1,5), union(2,4). Now sets: {0,2,4}, {1,3,5}. Edge (0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=0, they are same set, so conflict detected. Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find) Structure
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of maintaining disjoint sets with find and union operations
ladders.explanation: The Disjoint Set Union (DSU), or Union-Find, is a data structure that keeps track of a partition of elements into disjoint sets. It supports two primary operations: find(x), which returns the representative (root) of the set containing x, and union(x, y), which merges the sets containing x and y. We optimize find with path compression, making subsequent finds faster by flattening the tree. Union is optimized with union by rank, attaching the shorter tree under the taller one to keep the tree balanced. The intuition is that we can group elements dynamically and query their connectivity efficiently. For bipartite checking, we use DSU to group vertices into sets representing partitions. Time complexity for m operations on n elements is O(m α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant. Space complexity is O(n) for parent and rank arrays. This feeds into L2 by providing the underlying data structure to enforce constraints. Common mistakes include not initializing parent arrays correctly or implementing find without recursion leading to stack overflow. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)); rank = [0]*n; def find(x): while parent[x]!=x: parent[x]=parent[parent[x]]; x=parent[x]; return x; def union(x,y): rx,ry=find(x),find(y); if rx==ry: return; if rank[rx]<rank[ry]: parent[rx]=ry; elif rank[rx]>rank[ry]: parent[ry]=rx; else: parent[ry]=rx; rank[rx]+=1.

examples.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(2), union(1,3), find(0)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union by rank: parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, union: parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. find(0)=0, find(2)=2. union(1,3): find(1)=0, find(3)=2, union: rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1, so parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. find(0)=0.
Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union: parent[2]=0. find(3)=3.

testCases.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,3), find(0)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,3), find(3)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), find(5)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), find(2)
n=7, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), union(3,5), find(4)
n=7, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), union(3,5), find(6)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation to determine set representative with optimization
ladders.explanation: The find operation is a basic function in Union-Find that returns the root (representative) of the set containing an element x. Path compression is an optimization that flattens the tree structure during find operations by making each node point directly to the root. This reduces the height of the tree, making future finds faster. The intuition is that after finding the root for x, we update x's parent to the root, so subsequent finds for x or its ancestors become O(1). The algorithm recursively finds the root and sets parent[x] to it. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to path compression. Space complexity is O(1) extra per call. This feeds into L3 as a core component of the DSU structure. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if parent pointers are not updated correctly or using iterative find without compression. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x].

examples.trace: Initial parent array: index 0 parent 0, 1 parent 0, 2 parent 1, 3 parent 2, 4 parent 3. find(4): parent[4]=3 !=4, so call find(3). find(3): parent[3]=2 !=3, call find(2). find(2): parent[2]=1 !=2, call find(1). find(1): parent[1]=0 !=1, call find(0). find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Backtrack: set parent[1]=0, return 0; set parent[2]=0, return 0; set parent[3]=0, return 0; set parent[4]=0, return 0. Final parent = [0,0,0,0,0].
Initial parent: each node is its own parent. find(4): parent[4]=4, return 4. No compression needed.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets and Partitions
ladders.desc: Simplest prerequisite: understanding how elements can be grouped into disjoint sets
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the idea of partitioning a collection of elements into disjoint sets, where each element belongs to exactly one set, and sets do not overlap. In graph theory, this relates to grouping vertices into different categories, such as in bipartite graphs where vertices are divided into two sets with edges only between sets. The intuition is that we can model relationships by grouping related elements together. For example, if we have vertices 0, 1, 2, we might partition them into sets {0,2} and {1}. Basic operations include checking if two elements are in the same set and merging two sets. This is a fundamental concept in computer science used in clustering, connectivity, and more. Time complexity for naive implementations can be O(n), but we optimize with data structures like Union-Find. Space complexity is O(n) to track set membership. This feeds into L4 by providing the theoretical basis for set management. Common mistakes include confusing sets with lists or not ensuring disjointness. Real code pattern: Use arrays or dictionaries to represent set membership, e.g., set_id = [0,1,0] for elements 0,1,2 with sets 0 and 1.

examples.input: Elements: 0,1,2,3. Partition into sets: {0,2} and {1,3}. Check if 0 and 2 are in same set, merge sets {0,2} and {1,3}.
examples.output: 0 and 2 are same set; after merge: {0,1,2,3}
examples.trace: Initial partition: set_id = [0,1,0,1] where set_id[i] indicates set for element i. Check 0 and 2: set_id[0]=0, set_id[2]=0, so same set. Merge sets 0 and 1: update all elements with set_id 1 to 0: set_id becomes [0,0,0,0]. Now all elements in one set.

examples.input: Elements: a,b,c,d. Partition: {a,b}, {c}, {d}. Check if a and c are same set, merge {a,b} and {c}.
examples.output: a and c are different; after merge: {a,b,c}, {d}
examples.trace: Represent with map: set_id = {'a':0, 'b':0, 'c':1, 'd':2}. Check a and c: set_id['a']=0, set_id['c']=1, different. Merge sets 0 and 1: update 'c' to 0: set_id = {'a':0, 'b':0, 'c':0, 'd':2}.

testCases.input: Elements: 0,1, sets: {0}, {1}, check if 0 and 1 same set
Elements: 0,1, sets: {0,1}, check if 0 and 1 same set
Elements: 0,1,2, sets: {0,1}, {2}, check if 1 and 2 same set
Elements: 0,1,2, sets: {0,1}, {2}, merge sets, check if 0 and 2 same set
Elements: a,b,c, sets: {a,b}, {c}, check if a and b same set
Elements: a,b,c, sets: {a,b}, {c}, merge sets, check if b and c same set
Elements: x,y,z, sets: {x}, {y}, {z}, merge {x} and {y}, check if x and z same set
Elements: x,y,z, sets: {x}, {y}, {z}, merge all sets, check if y and z same set
Elements: 0,1,2,3, sets: {0,1}, {2,3}, check if 0 and 3 same set
Elements: 0,1,2,3, sets: {0,1}, {2,3}, merge sets, check if 1 and 2 same set
